subject: Product Management
title: The Five Whys
description: A 60-second lesson on The Five Whys.

hook.text: Toyota's Taiichi Ohno had a simple rule: when you find a problem, ask 'why' five times to find the root cause.

buildup.text: Why is the machine stopped? A fuse blew. Why? It overloaded. Why? The bearing wasn't lubricated. And so on.

discovery.text: By the fifth 'why,' you've moved from symptom to root cause — where the real fix lives.

twist.text: In product work, the Five Whys prevents building band-aid features. 'Users complain about speed' might trace to a data model flaw.

climax.text: The technique is deceptively simple. The discipline to keep asking 'why' instead of jumping to fixes is the hard part.

punchline.text: Five whys deep is where the real problem hides.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of the Five Whys technique?
options: To dig past symptoms and find the root cause of a problem
To ask users five questions in an interview
